text:
CT SCAN OF THE CHEST, ABDOMEN, AND PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST
ONCOLOGY SURVEILLANCE EXAMINATION

PATIENT: Margaret Osei-Bonsu
INDICATION: 48-year-old female with history of high-grade serous carcinoma of bilateral ovaries and fallopian tubes, FIGO stage IIIC (pT3c pN1 M0), germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant (5382insC). Prior primary debulking surgery with R0 resection, followed by six cycles of adjuvant Paraplatin/pacli with complete biochemical and radiographic response. Currently on nira (Zejula) maintenance therapy. This study performed for scheduled surveillance imaging at 12 months following completion of adjuvant chemotherapy, approximately 20 months from initial diagnosis. Comparison made to prior CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast obtained approximately 4 weeks after completion of cycle 6 chemotherapy.

TECHNIQUE: Helical CT acquisition through the chest, abdomen, and pelvis performed following the administration of 90 mL of Omnipaque-350 intravenous contrast material with portal venous phase imaging. Oral contrast was not administered. Slice thickness: 3 mm with 1.5 mm reconstructions. Images reviewed in multiple planes and windows.

---

FINDINGS:

CHEST:
Lungs and airways: The lungs are clear bilaterally. No pulmonary nodules, masses, or ground-glass opacities are identified. No new or enlarging parenchymal densities. The airways are patent to the subsegmental level bilaterally. No endobronchial lesions.

Pleurae: No pleural effusion bilaterally. No pleural thickening or nodularity. No pneumothorax.

Mediastinum and hila: The heart is normal in size. No pericardial effusion. The thoracic aorta is normal in caliber without aneurysm or dissection. No mediastinal widening. No paratracheal, prevascular, subcarinal, or hilar lymphadenopathy. The esophagus is unremarkable. No anterior mediastinal mass.

Bony thorax: No lytic or sclerotic osseous lesions to suggest metastatic disease. No acute rib fractures.

ABDOMEN:
Liver: The liver is normal in size and morphology with homogeneous parenchymal enhancement. No focal hepatic lesions are identified. No intrahepatic biliary ductal dilation. The portal and hepatic veins are patent.

Gallbladder and biliary system: The gallbladder is present, normal in size, without cholelithiasis, wall thickening, or pericholecystic fluid. No biliary ductal dilation.

Spleen: The spleen is normal in size (craniocaudal span approximately 9.2 cm) and homogeneous in attenuation. No focal splenic lesions.

Pancreas: The pancreas is normal in caliber and parenchymal attenuation throughout head, body, and tail. No pancreatic ductal dilation. No peripancreatic stranding or fluid.

Adrenal glands: Bilateral adrenal glands are normal in size and morphology. No adrenal nodules or masses.

Kidneys and ureters: Both kidneys are normal in size, position, and enhancement pattern. No hydronephrosis. No obstructing lithiasis. Note is made of a tiny, sub-5 mm hyperdense focus in the lower pole of the right kidney, most consistent with a tiny non-obstructing calculus; this is unchanged from the prior dedicated CT urogram obtained several months prior and is not hemodynamically significant. No urothelial thickening or upper tract lesions. No perirenal fluid or stranding.

Retroperitoneum and lymph nodes: The para-aortic and paracaval regions are evaluated throughout the abdomen. There is no retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy. The previously identified right para-aortic lymph node, which was involved with metastatic carcinoma at time of surgery, is not identified as a discrete enlarged node on this examination; no nodal recurrence in this region is seen. No mesenteric adenopathy.

Peritoneum and omentum: Particular attention was paid to the peritoneal surfaces, omental region, and mesentery given the patient's prior peritoneal carcinomatosis. The omentum appears thin and fatty without any soft tissue nodularity, irregular thickening, or confluent mass. No peritoneal nodules, plaques, or implants are identified along the right or left paracolic gutters, the diaphragmatic surfaces, the subhepatic and subphrenic spaces, the small bowel mesentery, the sigmoid mesentery, or the pelvic peritoneum. No ascites is present. The peritoneal fat planes are well-preserved and symmetric.

Small bowel and colon: Bowel loops are normal in caliber throughout. No wall thickening, obstruction, or mesenteric masses. No free intraperitoneal air. Post-surgical changes from prior bowel mobilization are stable.

PELVIS:
Post-hysterectomy and bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy changes are again noted, with expected absence of the uterus, cervix, ovaries, and fallopian tubes. The vaginal cuff appears intact without nodular thickening or adjacent mass. The pelvic soft tissues show expected post-operative remodeling without any abnormal soft tissue density, nodule, or mass lesion in the operative bed.

Bladder: The urinary bladder is well-distended and demonstrates normal wall thickness and smooth luminal surface. No filling defects.

Pelvic lymph nodes: No pelvic lymphadenopathy is identified. The bilateral external iliac, internal iliac, and obturator nodal chains are not enlarged. The previously noted right obturator lymphocele, which measured 2.1 cm on the immediate post-operative scan and had decreased to 0.8 cm on the pre-maintenance baseline CT, has fully resolved with no residual fluid collection identified on this examination.

Bony pelvis: No lytic or sclerotic osseous lesions in the visualized bony pelvis, sacrum, or proximal femora.

Limited visualization of the lumbar spine does not reveal any suspicious lesions.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. No evidence of disease recurrence. The peritoneal surfaces, omental region, retroperitoneum, and pelvic operative bed are entirely free of any lesion that would suggest recurrent high-grade serous carcinoma. This is a reassuring examination in a patient with prior FIGO stage IIIC peritoneal carcinomatosis who achieved complete gross resection.

2. Complete resolution of the right obturator lymphocele previously noted on post-operative and early surveillance imaging.

3. Tiny non-obstructing right lower pole renal calculus, stable and unchanged from the recent prior CT urogram, without hydronephrosis or urinary tract obstruction.

4. No pleural effusion, no pulmonary nodules, and no thoracic lymphadenopathy.

5. No abdominal or pelvic ascites.

Overall, these findings are consistent with a complete and sustained radiographic remission, concordant with the patient's persistently normal CA-125 values documented at recent clinical follow-up. The examination is otherwise unremarkable.
